text: The surgeon stands on the right side of the patient as a thirty-degree laparoscope is inserted through the umbilical port. The laparoscopic view shows the liver being carefully observed, and the peritoneal surface is explored for any signs of tumor metastasis.
The surgeon locates the sigmoid tumor and identifies the proximal resection margin, marking it with a surgical clip.
The surgeon retrieves free peritoneal fluid for cytology, and if needed for better exposure, suspends the uterus using a transfixed stitch.
Several interesting anatomical details are seen in this case.

The surgeon identifies the left ureter, inferior mesenteric vein, left colic vein, left colic artery, left kidney, left renal vein, and left gonadal vein. The dissection proceeds upwards to reveal the inferior pancreatic margin.
The surgeon follows the left colic vessels to reach the left colic flexure. The gastric body is visible through the transverse mesocolon.

The surgeon locates the aortic plane and identifies the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery. Several lymph nodes are visible in the area. On the right side of the aorta, the inferior vena cava is visualized.
The surgeon visualizes the promontorium while proceeding downwards, identifying the mesosigmoid, left ureter, and left gonadal vessels.

The surgeon visualizes the common iliac artery, external iliac artery, and hypogastric artery, along with the right ureter and right gonadal vessels near the pelvic inlet. The right ureter follows the superior aspect of the hypogastric artery, while the right gonadal vessels cross the right external iliac vessels. Additionally, the right external iliac artery, right external iliac vein, right round ligament of the uterus, right gonadal vessels, right uterine tube, right ureter, and right inguinal ligament are clearly visualized.

The surgeon identifies the path of the left common iliac vessels at the aortic bifurcation. Bilaterally, the ureters are visualized entering the pelvis, crossing the bifurcation of the common iliac vessels. The close proximity of the left ureter to the proximal tract of the inferior mesenteric artery is noted.

The surgeon identifies the intermesenteric plexus and begins dissection by dividing the peritoneum between the inferior mesenteric vein and the aorta. This approach facilitates the visualization and division of the atolletes fasciae, allowing the left ureter to be lowered and moved away from the inferior mesenteric artery for safer pursuit of the artery.
